ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for herbal spare rib rice. Mapped to pork rib dish (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible anchor; rice and herbal components are not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for the pork rib component of this mixed rice dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for herbal spare rib rice as a composite dish. A0FJP (cooked pork) is used as the primary protein anchor. The rice base and herbal ingredients are not individually coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish. SNOMED CT coverage of prepared Asian rice dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of herbal spare rib rice as served at a Singapore hawker centre or food court typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice topped with braised pork spare ribs and herbal broth.

nutrient_source.title: Herbal Spare Rib Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Herbal Spare Rib Rice (药膳排骨米饭), cr_id G00010915. All per-100g macros, micronutrients, and minerals are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Herbal Spare Rib Rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated using white rice (jasmine/long-grain, cooked) as the dominant carbohydrate source, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked white rice (GI range 64–80, representative value 72). The pork rib and herbal components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 75.2g available carb in 350g serving) / 100 ≈ 54.1. Available carbohydrate per 100g = 25.19 − 3.7 = 21.49g; per 350g serving = 75.2g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is borderline; partially offset by potassium at 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio favourable) and dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce in preparation to minimise sodium intake, monitor total meal sodium from accompaniments, and note that the potassium content from pork ribs and Chinese herbs (wolfberry, red dates) provides a meaningful cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~72) white rice base with net carbohydrate of approximately 21.5 g/100g creates a significant glycaemic load; dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g provides only partial glucose modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size of the rice component, consider requesting brown rice or cauliflower rice substitution to lower the glycaemic index, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the high glycaemic load per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork spare ribs contribute saturated fat (estimated ~1.45 g/100g) and the dish contains moderate sodium (231 mg/100g); however, dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g and potassium at 327 mg/100g are cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request leaner pork cuts or trimmed ribs to reduce saturated fat intake, limit soy sauce to control sodium, and benefit from the fibre and potassium content of the herbal components (wolfberry, red dates, astragalus) which support vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork spare ribs are a red meat source rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways relevant to RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise pork spare rib consumption due to arachidonic acid content; consider substituting with chicken or fish in a herbal preparation to retain the anti-inflammatory benefits of wolfberry, astragalus, ginger, and garlic while eliminating the primary inflammatory trigger.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Herbal Spare Rib Rice (药膳排骨米饭, Yào shàn pái gǔ mǐ fàn) is a traditional Chinese medicinal-cuisine dish popular in Singapore hawker centres and food courts. It consists of steamed white rice served with braised pork spare ribs that have been slow-simmered in a fragrant herbal broth incorporating Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) ingredients such as wolfberry (goji berries, 枸杞), red dates (jujube, 红枣), astragalus root (huang qi, 黄芪), ginger, and garlic, typically seasoned with soy sauce and Chinese cooking wine. The dish has a savoury, mildly sweet, and aromatic flavour profile with tender fall-off-the-bone pork ribs and soft, herb-infused rice. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 25.19g carbohydrate, 3.7g dietary fibre, 231mg sodium, 327mg potassium, 54mg calcium, 40mg magnesium, and 2.55mg iron. The high dietary fibre content (3.7g/100g) is notable for a rice-based dish and reflects the herbal ingredients. The dish carries a high glycaemic index (~72) due to the white rice base, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The potassium-to-sodium ratio is favourable for blood pressure management. The pork spare ribs contribute saturated fat and arachidonic acid, which are relevant considerations for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. The TCM herbal components (wolfberry, astragalus, red dates) provide antioxidant, immune-modulating, and adaptogenic properties recognised in traditional Chinese medicine practice.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010915) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The food identity is exact and the source match is direct.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g (1.45 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork spare rib cooked values proportioned to the fat content reported by HPB (4.21g total fat/100g); the HPB entry does not separately report saturated fat. This is documented as an adapted value. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010915 and is not available in any other approved source for this specific traditional composite dish preparation as of 2026-04-16: monounsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish; polyunsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish; trans_fat_g — trans fat not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_c_mg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_b6_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_b12_mcg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish; folate_mcg_dfe — folate not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_d_mcg — vitamin D not reported by HPB for this dish; vitamin_e_mg — vitamin E not reported by HPB for this dish; phosphorus_mg — phosphorus not reported by HPB for this dish; zinc_mg — zinc not reported by HPB for this dish; selenium_mcg — selenium not reported by HPB for this dish; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB for this dish. Source consulted and confirming unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/), entry G00010915, accessed 2026-04-16. These fields are set to -1 per the Veritas sentinel convention for data not available in approved sources. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is an unusually low value for a pork-containing dish and may reflect the composite dish average including the rice base diluting the cholesterol contribution. This value is preserved as reported by the approved source. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving (350g): available carbohydrate = (25.19 − 3.7) × 3.5 = 75.2g; GL = (72 × 75.2) / 100 = 54.1. 5. The dish is not certified halal as pork is a primary ingredient. 6. Ontology codes are approximate; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for this composite herbal rice dish.
